pages.text:
OPTIMUM THICKNESS FOR CHARCOAT IC INSULATOR COATING
INTRODUCTION
The usage of silicone rubber coa(cid:415)ngs for high voltage insulators has increased exponen(cid:415)ally in recent years and has
replaced the employment of greases and, to a large extent, spray washing, as a solu(cid:415)on to pollu(cid:415)on flashover prob-
lems. In spite of its popularity, however, confusion regarding the op(cid:415)mum thickness of the coa(cid:415)ng layer is clearly
evident.
Unfortunately, there is no interna(cid:415)onal or na(cid:415)onal standard that addresses this issue and thus a wide variety of
u(cid:415)lity and manufacturer’s specifica(cid:415)ons are in use. The only guidance from an independent organiza(cid:415)on is the IEEE
Standard No. 1523. Unfortunately, on the subject of coa(cid:415)ng thickness, the content of this document is contradictory
– some of the statements being:
“The nominal thickness suggested by several manufacturers has been 0.5mm. This thickness…has been used for years
in the coa(cid:415)ng industry and as such has li(cid:425)le significance for the coa(cid:415)ng of insulators”
“The experience to date suggests that coa(cid:415)ng thickness is not a factor in either the performance or life of the coat-
ing”.
“Coa(cid:415)ng thickness in the range of 0.125 to 0.7mm have been applied in the field with equal success”.
“Laboratory tests indicate that when leakage currents of a damaging magnitude develop on coa(cid:415)ngs, then thickness
plays a role in the coa(cid:415)ng life”.
“Thick coa(cid:415)ngs…do not allow the heat to be conducted away to the porcelain substrate as quickly as thinner coat-
ings”.
“Thick coa(cid:415)ngs can result in higher hot spot temperatures during dry band arcing, thereby causing thermal degrada-
(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng sooner than thinner coa(cid:415)ngs”.
“A coa(cid:415)ng that is too thin, however, can also degrade quickly due to wearing from environmental forces”.
“Tests indicate a thickness in the range of 0.38mm is op(cid:415)mum”.
It is clear from the above, and not surprising, that consensus on the best coa(cid:415)ng thickness to have has not been
reached.
COMMON COATING PRACTICE
Based on extensive tes(cid:415)ng at the coastal Koeberg Insulator Pollu(cid:415)on Tes(cid:415)ng Sta(cid:415)on operated by South Africa’s
na(cid:415)onal u(cid:415)lity, Eskom, and service experienced gained over a period of 26 years in all types of environment, we
consider that coa(cid:415)ng thicknesses adopted in most cases are too great. There are many reasons why thick layers are
applied, including:
• A general feeling that “thicker is be(cid:425)er”.
• Adop(cid:415)on of thicknesses tradi(cid:415)onally specified for corrosion protec(cid:415)on coa(cid:415)ngs
• Some coa(cid:415)ng types have a high viscosity owing to a large filler content and thus a thin layer is hard to achieve
• Manufacturers benefit by supplying larger volumes of material.
The effects of this are described below.

pages.text:
FILLER CONTENT
The amount of filler contained in the coa(cid:415)ng is a most important parameter. For good electrical performance and life,
the correct balance between silicone polymer – which dictates the hydrophobicity, hydrophobicity transfer and
hydrophobicity recovery rates – and filler – which protects the material from degrada(cid:415)on caused by surface par(cid:415)al
discharges – must be provided. There is, though, a tendency to include excess filler as it is significantly less expensive
than silicone and improves the results of ar(cid:415)ficial laboratory tracking and erosion tests which inten(cid:415)onally destroy
the hydrophobicity of the material under test. The thick layer naturally obtained with a coa(cid:415)ng containing a large
amount of filler will not perform as well as a thin layer with a higher propor(cid:415)on of silicone.
BONDING AGENT CONTENT
Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – hence its widespread use in the manufacture of
moulds. Thus, in most coa(cid:415)ngs, a bonding agent is included. This means that the percentage of silicone in the coa(cid:415)ng
is reduced and a significant volume of bonding agent is dispersed within the coa(cid:415)ng layer where it serves no benefi-
cial purpose. To further increase the silicone percentage, with the CharCoat IC system a primer is applied separately
prior to coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)on, therefore the bonding agent exists only where it is required, i.e. at the interface between
the coa(cid:415)ng and the porcelain, and no extraneous material is displacing the silicone content.
THERMAL CHARACTERISTICS
As stated in the IEEE Standard, to prevent premature aging of the coa(cid:415)ng, it is important that the surface hea(cid:415)ng
created by electrical discharges is efficiently conducted away. As silicone rubber has a high thermal resistance, the
coa(cid:415)ng thickness has an important influence on this. The diagrams below show the insulator temperature change
when coated and uncoated porcelain insulators are externally heated and cooled. This illustrates the high thermal
resistance of silicone rubber and why thermal effects need to be considered.

This illustrates the high thermal resistance of silicone rubber and why thermal effects need to be considered.

The thick layer naturally obtained with a coa(cid:415)ng containing a large amount of filler will not perform as well as a thin layer with a higher propor(cid:415)on of silicone. BONDING AGENT CONTENT Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – hence its widespread use in the manufacture of moulds. Thus, in most coa(cid:415)ngs, a bonding agent is included. This means that the percentage of silicone in the coa(cid:415)ng is reduced and a significant volume of bonding agent is dispersed within the coa(cid:415)ng layer where it serves no benefi- cial purpose. To further increase the silicone percentage, with the CharCoat IC system a primer is applied separately prior to coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)on, therefore the bonding agent exists only where it is required, i.e. at the interface between the coa(cid:415)ng and the porcelain, and no extraneous material is displacing the silicone content. pages.text:
In addi(cid:415)on to life extension, it has been found that thinner coa(cid:415)ngs also offer superior performance to thicker ones.
This was best illustrated by a PhD thesis undertaken by Dr. W L Vosloo of Eskom. His studies focused on the behavior
of insulators of iden(cid:415)cal shape and differing only in the material of their construc(cid:415)on. A comparison of the average
leakage current amplitudes recorded over a one-year period in the severe, natural environment of the Koeberg Test
Sta(cid:415)on is depicted below.
As can be noted from the diagram, the porcelain insulator coated with CharCoat IC displayed the lowest leakage
current ac(cid:415)vity. More surprising, however, is the fact that the daily maximum current amplitude on the CharCoat IC
porcelain was less than 35% of that of the silicon rubber composite unit. This large disparity is not due to their surface
proper(cid:415)es – both being fully hydrophobic – but due to their different thermal characteris(cid:415)cs. The thin coa(cid:415)ng layer
on a porcelain substrate allowed the temperature of the coated insulator to more closely track the changes in the
ambient temperature. The composite unit with no benefit from a porcelain heat sink experienced greater condensa-
(cid:415)on and consequent we(cid:427)ng of the surface contamina(cid:415)on, resul(cid:415)ng in increased leakage current flow.

His studies focused on the behavior of insulators of iden(cid:415)cal shape and differing only in the material of their construc(cid:415)on. A comparison of the average leakage current amplitudes recorded over a one-year period in the severe, natural environment of the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on is depicted below. As can be noted from the diagram, the porcelain insulator coated with CharCoat IC displayed the lowest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity. More surprising, however, is the fact that the daily maximum current amplitude on the CharCoat IC porcelain was less than 35% of that of the silicon rubber composite unit. This large disparity is not due to their surface proper(cid:415)es – both being fully hydrophobic – but due to their different thermal characteris(cid:415)cs. The thin coa(cid:415)ng layer on a porcelain substrate allowed the temperature of the coated insulator to more closely track the changes in the ambient temperature. The composite unit with no benefit from a porcelain heat sink experienced greater condensa- (cid:415)on and consequent we(cid:427)ng of the surface contamina(cid:415)on, resul(cid:415)ng in increased leakage current flow.

His studies focused on the behavior of insulators of iden(cid:415)cal shape and differing only in the material of their construc(cid:415)on. A comparison of the average leakage current amplitudes recorded over a one-year period in the severe, natural environment of the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on is depicted below. As can be noted from the diagram, the porcelain insulator coated with CharCoat IC displayed the lowest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity. More surprising, however, is the fact that the daily maximum current amplitude on the CharCoat IC porcelain was less than 35% of that of the silicon rubber composite unit. The composite unit with no benefit from a porcelain heat sink experienced greater condensa- (cid:415)on and consequent we(cid:427)ng of the surface contamina(cid:415)on, resul(cid:415)ng in increased leakage current flow. | Column 2 | Column 3 |

images.context_text: In addi(cid:415)on to life extension, it has been found that thinner coa(cid:415)ngs also offer superior performance to thicker ones. This was best illustrated by a PhD thesis undertaken by Dr. W L Vosloo of Eskom. His studies focused on the behavior of insulators of iden(cid:415)cal shape and differing only in the material of their construc(cid:415)on. A comparison of the average leakage current amplitudes recorded over a one-year period in the severe, natural environment of the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on is depicted below. As can be noted from the diagram, the porcelain insulator coated with CharCoat IC displayed the lowest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity. More surprising, however, is the fact that the daily maximum current amplitude on the CharCoat IC porcelain was less than 35% of that of the silicon rubber composite unit. This large disparity is not due to their surface proper(cid:415)es – both being fully hydrophobic – but due to their different thermal characteris(cid:415)cs. The thin coa(cid:415)ng layer on a porcelain substrate allowed the temperature of the coated insulator to more closely track the changes in the ambient temperature. The composite unit with no benefit from a porcelain heat sink experienced greater condensa- (cid:415)on and consequent we(cid:427)ng of the surface contamina(cid:415)on, resul(cid:415)ng in increased leakage current flow.

In addi(cid:415)on to life extension, it has been found that thinner coa(cid:415)ngs also offer superior performance to thicker ones. This was best illustrated by a PhD thesis undertaken by Dr. W L Vosloo of Eskom. His studies focused on the behavior of insulators of iden(cid:415)cal shape and differing only in the material of their construc(cid:415)on. A comparison of the average leakage current amplitudes recorded over a one-year period in the severe, natural environment of the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on is depicted below. As can be noted from the diagram, the porcelain insulator coated with CharCoat IC displayed the lowest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity. More surprising, however, is the fact that the daily maximum current amplitude on the CharCoat IC porcelain was less than 35% of that of the silicon rubber composite unit. This large disparity is not due to their surface proper(cid:415)es – both being fully hydrophobic – but due to their different thermal characteris(cid:415)cs. The thin coa(cid:415)ng layer on a porcelain substrate allowed the temperature of the coated insulator to more closely track the changes in the ambient temperature. The composite unit with no benefit from a porcelain heat sink experienced greater condensa- (cid:415)on and consequent we(cid:427)ng of the surface contamina(cid:415)on, resul(cid:415)ng in increased leakage current flow.

pages.text:
CHARCOAT IC FORMULATION
In view of the theore(cid:415)cal considera(cid:415)ons and prac(cid:415)cal test results described above, CharCoat IC was formulated with
three different solvent types to facilitate the easy achievement of a thin coa(cid:415)ng layer with a smooth surface to inhibit
contaminant accumula(cid:415)on. In addi(cid:415)on, silicone content was maximized by op(cid:415)mizing the silicone: filler ra(cid:415)o and
removing the bonding agent from the material.
APPLICATION PHILOSOPHY
Based on the valuable research conducted at Koeberg and subsequent experience from many sites in the field, the
recommended average thickness of an CharCoat IC coa(cid:415)ng layer to realise adequate heat dissipa(cid:415)on, was defined as
0.2mm a(cid:332)er complete curing. This translates to a coa(cid:415)ng usage of 1 litre per 2.3m2 of insulator surface.
Because of the fundamental geometry of the insulator, in prac(cid:415)ce there will always be varia(cid:415)on of the layer thickness
over the insulator surface. For example, in spraying the small valley between the ridges on the shed undersides,
considerably more coa(cid:415)ng will build up on the edges of the ridges and sheds. However, such layer non-uniformity has
negligible influence on the insulator performance. The coa(cid:415)ng is undertaken for the sole purpose of giving the insula-
tor hydrophobic proper(cid:415)es. These are not dependent on the coa(cid:415)ng thickness. Secondly, heat dissipa(cid:415)on relates
more to the average thickness than that at isolated points. It must also be appreciated that, if a minimum thickness
is specified, a major part of the insulator surface will have a thickness far above this value resul(cid:415)ng in wider thickness
varia(cid:415)on and much material wastage.
Measuring dry coa(cid:415)ng thickness – par(cid:415)cularly over many points to obtain a minimum or average – is difficult. Ultra-
sonic measurements are not very accurate and physical methods are destruc(cid:415)ve, necessita(cid:415)ng the need for subse-
quent touch-ups. Measuring thickness with the coa(cid:415)ng in a wet state is easier but not precise in defining the ul(cid:415)mate
dry thickness as the amount of solvent evapora(cid:415)on prior to the measurement is unknown. Moreover, the degree of
evapora(cid:415)on is highly dependent on the ambient temperature so the measured wet to dry thickness ra(cid:415)o will vary
with the ambient condi(cid:415)ons.
For these reasons, it is recommended that applica(cid:415)on control be exercised by measuring the total amount of materi-
al used per unit area of insulator surface.
TEST AND SERVICE EXPERIENCE
The formula(cid:415)on of CharCoat IC and its applica(cid:415)on method has proved to be successful by the excellent performance
experienced over more than 25 years on insulators ranging from 11kV to 765kVAC and 25kv to 533kVDC in the most
severe marine, industrial, desert and agricultural environments in Asia, Africa, Australasia, Europe, North America
and the Middle East.
To quan(cid:415)fy the performance of CharCoat IC in comparison to other coa(cid:415)ngs, reference can be made to the diagram
below. The graph indicates the accumulated leakage current ac(cid:415)vity recorded at the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on over a one
year period on three iden(cid:415)cal porcelain insulators – two of which were coated with different coa(cid:415)ngs and one le(cid:332)
uncoated. (Details of the test procedure and assessment method are defined in the Eskom Standard 34-224). In order
for a product to pass the test and gain approval for use on the Eskom system, no more than three flashovers are
permi(cid:425)ed over the one year test period.

rows: CHARCOAT IC FORMULATION In view of the theore(cid:415)cal considera(cid:415)ons and prac(cid:415)cal test results described above, CharCoat IC was formulated with three different solvent types to facilitate the easy achievement of a thin coa(cid:415)ng layer with a smooth surface to inhibit contaminant accumula(cid:415)on. In addi(cid:415)on, silicone content was maximized by op(cid:415)mizing the silicone: filler ra(cid:415)o and removing the bonding agent from the material. APPLICATION PHILOSOPHY Based on the valuable research conducted at Koeberg and subsequent experience from many sites in the field, the recommended average thickness of an CharCoat IC coa(cid:415)ng layer to realise adequate heat dissipa(cid:415)on, was defined as 0.2mm a(cid:332)er complete curing. This translates to a coa(cid:415)ng usage of 1 litre per 2.3m2 of insulator surface. Because of the fundamental geometry of the insulator, in prac(cid:415)ce there will always be varia(cid:415)on of the layer thickness over the insulator surface. For example, in spraying the small valley between the ridges on the shed undersides, considerably more coa(cid:415)ng will build up on the edges of the ridges and sheds. However, such layer non-uniformity has negligible influence on the insulator performance. The coa(cid:415)ng is undertaken for the sole purpose of giving the insula- tor hydrophobic proper(cid:415)es. These are not dependent on the coa(cid:415)ng thickness. Secondly, heat dissipa(cid:415)on relates more to the average thickness than that at isolated points. It must also be appreciated that, if a minimum thickness is specified, a major part of the insulator surface will have a thickness far above this value resul(cid:415)ng in wider thickness varia(cid:415)on and much material wastage. Measuring dry coa(cid:415)ng thickness – par(cid:415)cularly over many points to obtain a minimum or average – is difficult. Ultra- sonic measurements are not very accurate and physical methods are destruc(cid:415)ve, necessita(cid:415)ng the need for subse- quent touch-ups. Measuring thickness with the coa(cid:415)ng in a wet state is easier but not precise in defining the ul(cid:415)mate dry thickness as the amount of solvent evapora(cid:415)on prior to the measurement is unknown. Moreover, the degree of evapora(cid:415)on is highly dependent on the ambient temperature so the measured wet to dry thickness ra(cid:415)o will vary with the ambient condi(cid:415)ons. For these reasons, it is recommended that applica(cid:415)on control be exercised by measuring the total amount of materi- al used per unit area of insulator surface. TEST AND SERVICE EXPERIENCE The formula(cid:415)on of CharCoat IC and its applica(cid:415)on method has proved to be successful by the excellent performance experienced over more than 25 years on insulators ranging from 11kV to 765kVAC and 25kv to 533kVDC in the most severe marine, industrial, desert and agricultural environments in Asia, Africa, Australasia, Europe, North America and the Middle East. To quan(cid:415)fy the performance of CharCoat IC in comparison to other coa(cid:415)ngs, reference can be made to the diagram below. The graph indicates the accumulated leakage current ac(cid:415)vity recorded at the Koeberg Test Sta(cid:415)on over a one year period on three iden(cid:415)cal porcelain insulators – two of which were coated with different coa(cid:415)ngs and one le(cid:332) uncoated. (Details of the test procedure and assessment method are defined in the Eskom Standard 34-224). In order for a product to pass the test and gain approval for use on the Eskom system, no more than three flashovers are permi(cid:425)ed over the one year test period.

pages.text:
As shown in the Figure, the uncoated porcelain had the highest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity and experienced its fourth
flashover a(cid:332)er 6 months. Coa(cid:415)ng Type 2, a widely-used, thick, high filler content material complying fully with the
MEW, Kuwait and SEC, Saudi Arabia Specifica(cid:415)ons – failed a(cid:332)er suffering its fourth flashover in the ninth month.
CharCoat IC displayed far lower leakage current amplitudes and did not have a single flashover during the full year
test period. CharCoat IC is the only coa(cid:415)ng to have achieved this dis(cid:415)nc(cid:415)on to date.

Coa(cid:415)ng Type 2, a widely-used, thick, high filler content material complying fully with the MEW, Kuwait and SEC, Saudi Arabia Specifica(cid:415)ons – failed a(cid:332)er suffering its fourth flashover in the ninth month. CharCoat IC displayed far lower leakage current amplitudes and did not have a single flashover during the full year test period. CharCoat IC is the only coa(cid:415)ng to have achieved this dis(cid:415)nc(cid:415)on to date.
